items.q: Why do informal settlements develop in rapidly growing LIC cities?
items.correct: Rural-urban migration outpaces formal housing supply; migrants build their own homes on marginal land without secure land tenure — driven by housing shortage, not by personal choice.
items.misconception: People live in slums because they prefer them or are too lazy to find better housing.
items.random_wrong: Informal settlements are built by the government for new migrants.
items.why: Self-construction is a response to formal-sector failure. Many residents are employed and pay informal rent. Students moralise; the geographical analysis is about supply, tenure and land economics.

items.q: Why do meanders migrate across a floodplain?
items.correct: Faster water on the outside of the bend causes erosion (river cliff); slower water on the inside causes deposition (slip-off slope) — over time the channel shifts laterally.
items.misconception: Meanders form because rivers flow around obstacles like rocks.
items.random_wrong: Meanders form because the river is going downhill very steeply.
items.why: Meanders form on gentle gradients, not steep ones, and self-organise rather than dodging obstacles. Students project an intuitive 'water goes around things' model onto a fluid dynamic process.

items.q: Why do earthquakes of similar magnitude cause different amounts of damage?
items.correct: Damage depends on depth, distance from population, building quality, ground type (liquefaction risk), time of day, and emergency preparedness — not just magnitude.
items.misconception: Bigger magnitude always means more deaths.
items.random_wrong: All earthquakes of magnitude 7 cause the same damage everywhere.
items.why: Magnitude measures energy; impact depends on context. A magnitude 7 in a poor, dense city kills thousands; the same offshore or in a wealthy area may kill no one — a key development comparison.

items.q: Which African region suffers severe desertification?
items.why: It's Sahel. “Maghreb” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What is the maximum rainfall in hot deserts?
items.why: It's 250mm. “100mm” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What percentage of Northern Hemisphere is permafrost?
items.why: Exact answer: 25 %. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
